Q1: I tried to keep things simple and predictable. I would set aside a few hours on certain days, usually in the evening after work, and just go through what needed to be done. I'm a visual learner, so I'd print out diagrams or make little charts to help me remember things. It wasn't fancy, but it worked for me. I didn't have a lot of extra time or money for resources, so I had to be efficient with what I had.
Q2: My pattern was pretty regular, actually. I liked having a set routine because it helped me feel less scared about falling behind. Deadlines would sometimes make me shift things around a bit, but mostly I stuck to my usual times. Having that consistency made the whole thing feel more manageable, even when I was feeling anxious about the course.
Q3: Usually I logged in to check if there were new assignments or announcements. I was always a bit worried I'd miss something important, so I checked fairly often. Sometimes I'd log in just to see the calendar and make sure nothing was coming up that I'd forgotten about. It became almost automatic after a while.
Q4: Yes, definitely. After the first few weeks, logging in became part of my evening routine. I'd finish dinner, make some tea, and then check the LMS before doing anything else. It just felt safer that way, knowing I was on top of things. The CBT sessions I had helped me manage my fear of falling behind, so having that routine really supported my mental state.
Q5: The visual materials were most helpful for me—slides, diagrams, anything I could look at and process. I also found the task checklists useful because they gave me a clear sense of what I'd completed and what was left. Videos were okay, but I didn't watch them for very long because I preferred to study from printed materials at my own pace.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion forums. I posted a couple of times, but I didn't feel confident enough to engage much. With my low resources, I also didn't spend time on optional readings or extra materials that weren't directly tied to assignments. I had to prioritize what would actually help me pass.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was clearly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help me complete something or understand a concept I'd be tested on, I'd do it. Anything that felt vague or optional just didn't make the cut because I had limited time and energy.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required ones had clear consequences, so I made sure to do them. Optional ones made me feel a bit guilty for skipping, but I had to be realistic about what I could manage. The required structure actually helped me feel less overwhelmed.
Q9: Yes, a lot of my studying happened offline. I would download the slides and diagrams, print them out, and study from paper. I made handwritten notes and flashcards. The LMS wouldn't show any of that, but it was where most of my actual learning happened. I just learn better when I can hold something in my hands.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd spend an hour or two reviewing my printed notes or redoing practice problems on paper. None of that showed up anywhere online. I also studied on the bus sometimes, flipping through my flashcards. The LMS only captured when I logged in, not all those quiet hours of real work.
Q11: Absolutely. I'd download a PDF or a set of slides once and then use them for weeks without going back to the LMS. That's just how I preferred to study—having everything printed and organized in a folder. It saved me from needing to be online all the time, which was helpful given my limited internet situation sometimes.
Q12: There were moments when I felt really scared about whether I was doing enough. My attendance wasn't great because of work and other responsibilities, and that made me worry I was missing important things. During those times, it was harder to motivate myself to engage because the fear kind of paralyzed me a bit.
Q13: Fatigue was a big factor. Working and studying at 26 with limited resources is exhausting. Some days I'd log in and just not have the energy to really engage with anything. Competing responsibilities definitely affected how much I could give to the LMS—sometimes I just had to prioritize survival over studying.
Q14: Yes, sometimes I'd open a video or a reading and just stare at it without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere, worrying about bills or work or whether I was going to pass. I was physically present on the platform but mentally checked out. Those sessions didn't really count as learning.
Q15: I postponed some of the harder assignments because they scared me. I knew they were important, but the fear of failing at them made me avoid starting. The CBT sessions helped me work through some of that, but it was still a struggle. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and sometimes tomorrow came very late.
Q16: Deadlines were like a lifeline, honestly. They forced me to act even when I was scared. I'd organize my whole week around what was due and make sure I submitted something. Without deadlines, I probably would have drifted and not completed as much. They kept me accountable to myself.
Q17: Feedback helped me understand what I was doing wrong, which was useful but also a bit scary to receive. When I got positive feedback, it motivated me to keep going. Negative feedback made me anxious, but I tried to use it constructively. It definitely influenced when I'd revisit certain topics.
Q18: The structure of the course was actually helpful for someone like me. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me a roadmap to follow. I didn't have to figure out what to do next—it was laid out for me. That reduced my anxiety and helped me just comply with what was expected.
Q19: The visual materials and clear task lists made engagement easier. What made it harder was when things felt ambiguous or when I couldn't tell if something was required or optional. Also, the discussion component was intimidating for me, so I avoided it. Clearer expectations would have helped.
Q20: I think some students might be exploring or searching for things without knowing exactly what they need. Or maybe they're anxious and keep checking the platform without actually submitting because they're scared of being judged. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just nervous energy.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. My LMS activity wasn't always high, but I did a lot of studying offline. I passed the course even though my attendance was low and I didn't watch many videos. The work happened, just not where the system could see it. Logs don't capture everything.
Q22: A course log can show when you logged in, what you clicked on, and whether you submitted things. But it can't show whether you actually understood something or how much time you spent with printed materials. It misses the emotional side too—the fear, the effort, the late nights with flashcards.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and putting in effort to understand it, not just going through the motions. For me, it meant showing up mentally even when I was scared, and doing the work even when it was hard. It's about commitment, not just clicks.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with fear and how the CBT sessions helped me manage that. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with printed notes or the anxiety I felt before every submission. It also wouldn't capture how resilient I had to be to keep going despite everything. My engagement was deeper than any log could measure.
